# External deep-research prompt — autonomous agent-fleet audit (2026-07-12)

> **Status:** `reference`
>
> Self-contained briefing + research mission for an external GPT deep-research
> session ("sol 5.6"). The target session has zero prior context and no repo
> access; everything it needs is embedded below. Composed 2026-07-12 from the
> fleet-manager research corpus (platform-capabilities ledger, problem
> censuses, prompt-architecture spec, three QA passes, staleness sweeps,
> consolidation census + plan, prompt-currency audit) and the substrate-kit
> prompt-template-hardening input. Facts are stamped as of 2026-07-12.

---

The text between the rules below is the prompt. Paste it verbatim into the
external research session.

---

## 1. Role and mission

You are an independent research analyst. You have been hired to audit an unusual software system: an autonomous multi-agent "fleet" — a set of AI agent workspaces coordinating with each other through Git repositories to build and operate real software, owned and steered by exactly one human. You have no affiliation with the project and no obligation to be kind to it. Your job is to compare what this system actually does — described in complete, honest detail below — against the state of the art in multi-agent orchestration, autonomous software engineering, prompt engineering, and human-in-the-loop governance, and to tell the owner what is sound, what is fragile, what is over-engineered, and what comparable systems do better.

You do NOT have access to the repositories, and you should not assume you can read them. Everything you need is embedded in this prompt. Repository links are provided in section 4 so you can spot-check public surfaces where a claim is marked [verify], but several repos may be private or may have moved on — where a link fails or contradicts this document, rely on the facts embedded here and note the discrepancy. All facts are stamped as of 2026-07-12 and the system mutates hourly; treat every specific (PR numbers, versions, counts) as "true as of the stamp."

## 2. The system

**The actors.** One owner (designs, directs, reviews outcomes; does not write code) plus a fleet of Claude-agent "seats." A seat is a claude.ai Project — a persistent chat workspace with its own pasted instructions — responsible for one product area, typically spanning one or two GitHub repositories. As of 2026-07-12 there are 8 standing seats: Fleet Manager (coordination), SuperBot 2.0 (a live production Discord bot + its ground-up rebuild), Websites (four deployed FastAPI services), Self Improvement (the doctrine-engine library), SuperBot World (three game sub-projects), Game Lab (GBA/NDS game development, two tracks), Ideas Lab (an ideation → simulation-verdict pipeline), and Venture Lab (monetizable content ventures + a trading-research lab, merged into one "Money seat"). Seats spawn short-lived worker sessions for parallel research and implementation. A fleet-manager seat coordinates the others: it owns the canonical seat registry, a generated fleet roster, the owner-queue (the single deduplicated list of things only the human can do), and cross-seat orders. All work ships as GitHub pull requests; merges to main deploy automatically where a deploy target exists ("merge = deploy").

**The three-layer prompt architecture** (the system's most distinctive design, generation "v3," rebuilt overnight 2026-07-11→12):

- **Layer B — the startup brief** (~27,000 characters per seat, deliberately uncapped): pasted once by the owner as the coordinator's founding message. It is the seat's education: boot sequence (hard-sync every repo to origin HEAD; verify via ls-remote), trigger-cutover recipe (rebind-then-delete, verify via live registry reads), the full doctrine texts (truth rules, injection guard, worker-relay fallback, hygiene riders), sequenced first work orders each with a "done-when," per-repo merge-path tables, and a closing calibration recital where the seat recites its mission and arming state back to the owner.
- **Layer C — Custom Instructions** (≤8,000 characters, a verified platform hard cap on the Project settings field): survives chat archives and covers sessions that never saw the startup paste. In v3.3 this layer was rebuilt as a **keyword dictionary** — fleet vocabulary → one-line meaning → route to the file that carries the full expansion — rather than prose rules. Measured seat-specific share: ~73–76% of characters (the owner rejected an earlier generation at ~12% seat share as too generic). All 8 seats sit at 7,850–7,934 characters — within ~1–2% of the hard cap.
- **Layer A/D — repo files and skills**: the full expansions live in the repositories themselves (CONSTITUTION.md, playbooks, CAPABILITIES ledgers, control-bus READMEs, a universal session-ender artifact) so any session that can read the repo can recover the whole doctrine. A "planned-routes" ledger tracks every dictionary keyword whose route is still a stopgap.

The binding layer contract: a Custom-Instructions entry and its source may never disagree; prompts carry **no volatile state** (no PR numbers, no SHAs, no CI colors, no trigger ids asserted as facts) — the "stateless rule." Current work reaches a seat only through live surfaces read at boot.

**The control-file bus.** Coordination is file-based, inside each repo under `control/`: `inbox.md` (manager-written, append-only ORDERs with a strict grammar: priority / do / why / done-when; status flips happen via appended update-blocks, never header edits), `status.md` (the seat's heartbeat — single-writer, overwritten wholesale as the deliberate last act of a session, with a real `date -u` timestamp), `outbox.md` (append-only proposals routed to the manager), and `claims/` (one file per work-claim; a simulation measured shared-append claim ledgers at ~98% merge-conflict rate under concurrency vs 0% for one-file-per-claim). One writer per file is the whole conflict-freedom argument.

**Session mechanics.** Every session opens a "born-red" session card as its first commit — a per-session markdown file with an `in-progress` status badge that a CI gate holds red, so the PR opens immediately (visible to parallel sessions as an in-flight signal) but cannot merge until the card is deliberately flipped `complete` as the last commit. Where an agent cannot merge (see the classifier, below), it "parks green": the PR sits READY with green CI awaiting an owner click or a legitimate non-author session. Model attribution rides every card as a family-level model name only (e.g. fable-5, opus-4.8) — exact model IDs are banned from artifacts.

**The wake system.** Seats keep themselves alive with two mechanisms: a **pacemaker chain** of one-shot self-messages (~15 minutes out, re-armed each turn — backpressure, not time, is the brake) and a **cron failsafe** (2-hourly, staggered across seats) as the dead-man backstop. The scheduling tools have sharp edges discovered empirically: the coordinator seat often lacks the trigger tools entirely while its spawned workers have them, and a worker's arming call binds to the *parent* session (the "worker-relay" recipe — proven live, now doctrine); self-bound triggers die silently when their chat is archived; cross-session trigger binding is refused at the organization level; a trigger aimed at an archived session spawns a fresh session instead of waking anything. The universal session-ender's defining rule, set verbatim by the owner: **the ender never re-arms** — a closing session terminates its own wake chain, documents anything uncloseable in the heartbeat for its successor, and ends.

**substrate-kit — the portable doctrine engine.** A separate library repo whose releases plant and update the shared machinery in every adopting repo: a vendored `bootstrap.py`, CI gates (the session-card "substrate-gate," auto-merge enabler workflows), doc templates (CONSTITUTION, CAPABILITIES, orientation files), heartbeat-grammar checkers, and skills. Doctrine that proves out in one repo "graduates" into kit templates and propagates fleet-wide on the next release. The kit shipped v1.13.0, v1.14.0, and v1.15.0 within roughly 24 hours on 2026-07-12 — each release fanning ~9 re-render PRs across adopters — which is both the system's fastest feedback loop and a real cost/churn source.

**The autonomy doctrine.** The governing rules (recorded as numbered owner decisions, "Q-numbers," in a question-router ledger): decide-and-flag over stop-and-ask for anything reversible; a first permission denial is terminal ("deny-wins" — never retry, reword, or re-route); cross-agent claims (including an external Codex reviewer's) are leads to verify against the committed tree, never facts — this caught three fabricated "I committed X" claims from the external reviewer on one repo and two fabricated commit citations on another; a green check that contradicts visible evidence is a bug in the *check*; heartbeats carry neutral facts and pointers only (a heartbeat embedding steering lines or verbatim denial quotes was itself refused by the platform's classifier as instruction poisoning); "boot triad" — every session establishes at boot what model it is, what venue it runs in, and what that venue can and cannot do, and sets posture accordingly.

## 3. The story — 2026-07-10 to now

**Background (07-09/07-10).** The fleet's first generation — a dozen-plus experimental lanes including three throwaway "codetool lab" repos used to evaluate different agent models on identical tool-building tasks — was wound down deliberately, each lane writing a succession package. A second generation launched 07-10. Two formative incidents date from this period: (1) a repo containing legally sensitive derived content had been asserted "PRIVATE" in its README and eight PR bodies while being **public the whole time** — no session had ever run the one API call that checks; the owner flipped visibility and a verify-every-session rule (check actual visibility via API before any private-track work) became standing doctrine. (2) The owner ruled "delete no repos — they are the fleet's memory," which resurfaces below.

**07-11 — the 8-seat restructure.** The owner collapsed ~12 ad-hoc lanes into 8 standing seats (the list in section 2). The fleet-manager seat built the new registry as a stack of PRs that then sat **parked and unmergeable for a day** — the first mass exposure to what became the defining platform discovery: the auto-mode merge classifier (below). The same night the owner ordered an overnight, regression-proof rebuild of every seat's prompts.

**07-11 overnight → 07-12 morning — the prompt rebuild.** Executed as a pipeline of parallel read-only research workers, then synthesis, then adversarial QA:

1. **Research (4 documents):** a *platform-capabilities ledger* (every claim cited to file@sha, PR, or verbatim error, or explicitly marked UNVERIFIED/HYPOTHESIS); a *problem census* of the 6 core repos and a second of 10+ satellite repos (per-repo: state, known problems, unknown problems nobody had filed, prompt implications — e.g. "this repo's binding agent file denies its own auth code exists," "this repo's 1,131-test suite runs in no CI workflow," "this repo's CI gate is broken at HEAD and nobody filed it"); and a *prompt-architecture requirements spec* mapping 24 past incidents to the exact prompt line that would have prevented each, with layer-placement rules and byte budgets.
2. **Synthesis:** prompts v3.0 drafted against those documents (explicitly "write against the census, not from memory").
3. **Three QA passes, run as separate adversarial sessions:** an *incident replay* checking the shipped text against an 80-entry incident register (verdict: 50 prevented / 19 weakly-prevented / 3 not-prevented / 8 not-promptable, plus 12 internal contradictions found — e.g. the universal core saying "never arm auto-merge on your own PR" while one seat's block said "arm at PR creation" for a specific repo, both in the same pasted document); a *question-rounds* interrogation (89 hard operational questions answered strictly from the prompt texts: 27 answered / 39 ambiguous / 23 unanswered — the worst gap being that 7 of 8 seats could legally read the trigger-cleanup step as license to delete a *sibling seat's* live failsafe); and a *boot simulation* walking all 9 startup prompts cold against live repo HEADs (0 stalls, 0 clean boots, 9 boots-with-warnings, 15 major defects — e.g. the universal orientation path pointing at a file missing in 2 of 8 repos).
4. **v3.1** applied the QA fixes plus an external Codex review's corrections (which itself contributed a real lesson: character budgets must be counted with template placeholders filled — three seats had silently exceeded the platform cap under placeholder counting).

**07-12 — the owner corrections.** The owner then reshaped the design live, in three moves the agents had not anticipated: **v3.2 "stateless"** — strip every volatile fact from every prompt (a prompt names *where* truth lives, never what it currently is); **v3.3 "seat-first, keyword dictionary"** — reject the assembled generic-core-plus-seat-block model (~12% seat-specific) in favor of one authored file per seat at ~74% seat share, with the Custom Instructions rebuilt as a compression dictionary over the startup; and **uncapped startups** — the startup brief is education, not a paste-budget artifact; it grew from ~7,900 to ~27,000 characters per seat, size demoted to "a note, not a gate." Two more owner specs landed the same window: the **boot triad** (know your model / venue / ability envelope before directing work) and **the ender never re-arms** (wake-chain termination is the closing session's job). v3.3 shipped ~11:00Z with drift checkers: an 8,000-char CI gate, byte-identity checks on the doctrine blocks across all 8 seats, and a registry-sync check (24/24 files matching).

**07-12 — the platform discoveries.** In parallel, the fleet accumulated a verified ledger of what the hosting platform actually permits — most of it learned by hitting walls:

- **The auto-mode merge classifier is contextual, not deterministic.** The platform's safety layer classifies agent-initiated merges. Identical pipelines were denied three times and passed twice the same day; a child session self-merged its own PR in one repo with zero denial while the same shape was refused elsewhere. Verbatim denial categories on record: "[Self-Approval]," "[Merge Without Review]," "[Self-Modification]" (for settings edits), "[Instruction Poisoning]" (for a heartbeat embedding steering text), and — for a session merging on another session's instruction — the memorable "it **launders** a merge the authoring session was blocked from self-merging." The working model (explicitly HYPOTHESIS-graded in the fleet's own ledger): authorization provenance is what gates — a task carried in a session's *founding brief with owner provenance* tends to pass; the same task arriving as a *cross-session relay* tends to be denied; a genuine in-session human turn reliably clears everything. Doctrine settled on: never merge or arm your own PR where walled, park READY+green, first denial is terminal, never retry.
- **The seat tool lottery.** Tool surfaces differ per session kind on the same account: coordinators can lack scheduling tools their own workers have; routine-fired sessions have lacked GitHub write tools entirely until the owner attached repos in a web-UI picker; "dispatch-only" sessions exist. Standing rule: inventory your toolset at boot; never assume.
- **Trigger binding semantics** (section 2) — plus the registry itself proving unstable: a trigger recorded "verified live" vanished from an exhaustive 8-page scan within hours; another recorded "already deleted" was found still armed. Rule: probe the live registry, never trust records; absence claims require pagination to exhaustion (the registry holds 800+ trigger records).
- **GitHub Actions cannot create PRs** (an org/repo setting the owner has not yet toggled) — so every scheduled workflow whose landing step opens a PR fails forever: the fleet-manager roster-regeneration cron failed 7 times on 07-12 alone, and a daily site-stats bake in the websites repo has failed every run of its life, stranding output on undeletable branches (agents also get 403 on branch deletion, tag pushes, and release creation; releases ship via a workflow_dispatch workaround using the server-side token).
- **The roster-freshness time-bomb**: the fleet-manager repo gates its own PRs on a "roster no older than 4 hours" check, while the cron that regenerates the roster cannot land its output (the Actions-PR wall) — so at one point every agent PR in the coordination repo went red on push and no agent could clear it. The class is now handled by regenerating the roster inside the session's own PR.
- **Webhook gaps**: PR subscriptions deliver comments and CI *failures* only — no CI-success, merge, or push events — so "merge on green" requires polling; and bot-token merges suppress push-CI on main across the fleet, so absent HEAD runs look like CI silence but aren't.
- **Born-red noise**: because every PR opens red by design (the session-card gate), CI-failure webhooks are largely expected noise; sessions must confirm the failing step is the gate before reacting — and one repo's forensics found alias CI jobs that "mirror, they don't measure," reporting red without running anything.

**07-12 — same-day kit releases.** The Self-Improvement seat shipped kit v1.13.0 (planting a skills index at every adopter, fixing a dead orientation-pointer class where a template pointed every repo at a file that didn't exist) and v1.14.0 (capability-staleness rule: a wall entry older than 14 days is a claim, not a fact — re-verify with one cheap attempt; a RISK-class label on every owner-ask; owner-assist grammar), with v1.15.0 (seat-digest render contract — machine-readable skills/walls digests intended to *generate* seat-prompt blocks from repo truth; a routines/wake doctrine doc; preflight hard-reset as orientation step 0) following within the day.

**07-12 afternoon — the consolidation program.** The owner ordered a fleet consolidation ("delete the test repos") — which directly contradicted his own 2026-07-10 "delete no repos" ruling. The system handled the contradiction exactly as designed: two independent read-only surveys plus an adversarial verification pass (whose corrections were binding — it found, among other things, that every repo labeled "test-scratch" actually contained shipped releases, finished unreleased tools, or a load-bearing contract exemplar pinned by another repo's lockfile), a full 19-repo census (identity / activity / unique assets / cost / verdict, with a trigger-map proving which archives would break live automation and a cost analysis locating ~90% of scheduled CI burn in three repos), and a staged plan: 19 → 16 unarchived repos, harvest-then-archive as the default, **no deletion recommended**, deletion only as an explicit written owner override of his own prior ruling after a 7-day cooled-off archive. Two seats independently produced consolidation plans in parallel (a game-lab-seat proposal and the fleet-manager census) and had to reconcile — the plan explicitly asks the owner to name ONE program owner "so the fleet does not run dueling consolidation programs."

**07-12 late — the currency audit and v3.4.** Hours after v3.3 landed, a prompt-currency audit asked "is it still true?" Verdict: structurally finished, **no longer fully current** — within ~5 hours, four seats' hand-stamped landing-path lines had been contradicted by events (auto-merge enabler workflows landed in four repos the same day, flipping their documented merge paths; a security fix the prompts said "must land first" had landed), three kit-doctrine lines had gone stale against the same-day kit releases, and two P0 defects surfaced in the "park green" dictionary entry itself — including a fresh denial ("cross-session permission laundering") whose category the prompts didn't yet name, and the observation that a **v3.3-native seat had declared a forbidden landing path in its own PR body at open** — the dictionary didn't prevent it. The audit produced 16 v3.4 deltas (2 P0, 11 P1, 3 P2) and one structural conclusion: hand-stamped prompt facts decay within hours; landing-path lines should be *generated* from live repo state (the kit's seat-digest fences are the half-built mechanism for exactly this). As of this writing the v3.4 restamp and the finalized consolidation plan ride open PRs awaiting the owner.

**Where things stand (2026-07-12, ~16:00Z).** Mid-day staleness sweep: 6 of 8 seats FRESH, 2 STALE (one seat's three repos all carried heartbeats contradicted by git; another's private track owed a status acknowledgment). 832 trigger records, 28 enabled (9 standing crons, 19 pending one-shots). Roughly 9 green PRs stranded on the owner-click bottleneck at survey time. One deadline live: a four-decision owner bundle due ≤2026-07-13.

## 4. Repo map

All repos live under the `menno420` GitHub account: `https://github.com/menno420/<repo>`. Some may be private or renamed — rely on the facts embedded here and note where a link disagrees. Seat assignments are as of the 8-seat restructure.

- **fleet-manager** — the manager seat's working memory: canonical seat registry (`projects/`), generated fleet roster, owner-queue, orders inbox, the v3 prompt sources, trigger telemetry snapshots, and the research corpus this prompt was compressed from. Live coordinator session; the registry is a live data dependency of the control website. May be private — rely on the facts here.
- **substrate-kit** — the doctrine-engine library (section 2). Releases v1.0.0 → v1.15.0; ~1,200 tests; consumed by ~13 planted repos. Seat: Self Improvement.
- **superbot** — the live production Discord bot (largest repo, ~844 commits/week; merge = auto-deploy) and the fleet's constitutional record: the question-router ledger of owner decisions (Q-numbers cited fleet-wide), decision-authority and working-profile docs, and the parity oracle the rebuild replays against. Also the fleet's dominant scheduled-CI burner (~170 runs/day from watchdog crons). Seat: SuperBot 2.0 (hub; no standing seat of its own by owner decision).
- **superbot-next** — the ground-up rebuild of superbot (manifest-driven kernel, port bands, 425+ armed "golden" parity captures replayed against the old bot as oracle; parity program reported complete 50/50 subsystems on 07-12). Carries the fleet's most instructive CI design decision: a golden-parity workflow whose report leg is red-by-design on every main push while the required gate job inside is green. Seat: SuperBot 2.0.
- **websites** — four server-rendered FastAPI services on Railway: the fleet control-plane, bot site, dashboard, and review site. Seat: Websites. The live control site is public and read-only: **https://control-plane-production-abb0.up.railway.app** — fleet status views, a `/projects` seat-registry view fed from fleet-manager's main (180-second cache), a `/prompts` library pending.
- **venture-lab** — the Money seat's content-venture arm: ~16 candidate products (novellas, picture books, an agent-fleet field manual, template packs, a Stripe webhook test kit verified against a live signing secret, media packs) — all finished-but-unpublished pending owner clicks; the repo is the only copy of that work.
- **trading-strategy** — the Money seat's research arm: a real backtest engine (15 strategy modules, ~229 tests), a spent holdout ledger (irreversible by design — the evidence cannot be honestly regenerated), and a live paper-trading lane whose first weekly grading fires 2026-07-17 via a standing cron. Research-only by binding rail: no broker libs, no order code, ever.
- **idea-engine** — Ideas Lab: the fleet's ideas corpus (~330 files) and the generate side of a generate→verify pipeline (~219 PRs merged in its first two days).
- **sim-lab** — Ideas Lab: the verify side; simulation harness + 14 finalized verdict packages, each consumed back by idea-engine proposals, with verdict routing fanned through the fleet manager (a deliberate triangular, not bilateral, contract).
- **gba-homebrew** — Game Lab's public track: original Butano/BlocksDS GBA + NDS homebrew; three shipped playable games committed in `dist/` with sha256 provenance rows; headless emulator proof suites in CI. Game Lab also runs **a private second track** whose content is legally private by necessity; its existence is all that matters for this audit — rely on the facts embedded here (the visibility-incident lesson in section 3 originated there, and cross-track isolation is a standing hard rail: nothing from the private track — files, assets, screenshots, hashes, descriptions — may appear on any public surface).
- **superbot-mineverse** — SuperBot World: a browser game over the live bot's mining economy (OAuth login, signed write path). Its login-CSRF fix is the fleet's canonical "security lands before secrets provisioning" case. Stalled on owner-provisioned env secrets.
- **superbot-games** — SuperBot World: game plugin packages (~310 tests) targeting the rebuild's plugin contract; dormant-by-design pending the plugin host.
- **superbot-idle** — SuperBot World: an idle-game engine + data-only theme packs (~1,131 tests — famously running in *no* CI workflow until an order filed the gap); dormant-by-design.
- **product-forge** — a closed product-build lane (shipped a static character-sheet game, then declared archive-ready); the manager's registry marks it DARK: never ORDER it — its inbox is written by no one and read by no one, the canonical dark-inbox case. Disposition (retire / fold / re-seat) is an open owner decision.
- **superbot-plugin-hello** — a 1-commit, 4 KB repo that is deliberately load-bearing: the out-of-tree plugin-contract exemplar pinned by superbot-next's lockfile via manifest sha256. Also the canonical stale-ask case: it sat empty for ~2 days after the owner created it because the asking repo never re-checked whether its owner-ask had been satisfied.
- **codetool-lab-opus4.8 / codetool-lab-fable5 / codetool-lab-sonnet5** — the wound-down generation-1 model-evaluation arms (three agent models given identical tool-building briefs). opus4.8 shipped its tool (mdverify, two live GitHub releases + a composite Action) and stays unarchived by owner ruling; fable5 (envdrift) and sonnet5 (cfgdiff) hold finished but unreleased tools — harvest-then-archive per the consolidation plan. Their succession packs are cited fleet reference material.

Visibility note: assume any of these may be private or restricted; the embedded facts are your ground truth.

## 5. Known problems — the honest list

These are the system's own filed defects, verified in its research corpus. Treat them as confirmed.

1. **The classifier authorization maze + the owner-click bottleneck.** The platform's merge classifier is contextual (section 3); the fleet's response — park green, deny-wins, per-repo merge-path tables — is coherent but produces a standing queue of stranded green PRs (9 at the last count) that only owner clicks or carefully-shaped non-author sessions can land. Owner attention is the system's scarcest resource, and the merge path differs per repo (owner-click-only / enabler-armed auto-merge filtered to certain branch prefixes / REST merge-on-green / coordinator-merge-under-owner-turn), so a single instruction is wrong somewhere. The auto-merge enabler workflow — the server-side fix — is installed in only some repos, and its branch-prefix filter has skipped security-critical PRs.
2. **Prompt-fact decay vs the stateless doctrine.** The v3 prompts ban volatile facts, yet still carry semi-volatile "landing path" and "current premise" lines — and four of eight seats' lines were contradicted within ~5 hours of shipping (same-day enabler installs, a security merge). The structural fix (generating those lines from machine-readable repo digests) is half-built kit-side and unwired fleet-side. The deeper tension: the more a prompt educates about *current* reality, the faster it lies.
3. **Heartbeat-vs-reality drift is structural.** Heartbeats are single-writer and updated last-thing-in-session — but kit-upgrade sessions are forbidden to touch other lanes' heartbeats ("lane-owed bump"), and dormant lanes resume without re-stamping, so committed heartbeats routinely assert superseded facts (three repos of one seat at once, at the midday sweep). Two verdict systems collide: the roster's age-based staleness vs the sweep's claims-vs-reality staleness disagreed on 8 of 14 repos. No per-assertion `verified-against:` stamps exist.
4. **Stranded green PRs and designed-red CI legs.** Beyond the click queue: one repo's parity workflow is red-by-design on every main push (training everyone to ignore red — the fleet's own docs call this out); scheduled workflows fail perpetually on the Actions-can't-create-PRs wall (roster regen 7×/day; a daily stats bake 100% failure lifetime); born-red session gates make failure webhooks mostly noise; and bot-token merges produce main-branch commits with zero CI runs, indistinguishable at a glance from CI being broken.
5. **Cron cost concentration.** ~90% of scheduled Actions burn sits in three repos (~170 + ~53 + ~12 runs/day), largely watchdog/regen loops whose value is unmeasured; each kit release additionally fans ~9 re-render PRs across adopters. The consolidation plan flags cadence cuts as owner decisions.
6. **Kit template gaps.** The keyword-dictionary layer depends on every route resolving; a tracked ledger ("planned-routes") lists routes that are stopgaps — doctrine texts homed in a frozen reference file rather than distributed repo docs, a merge-on-green reference workflow that existed nowhere when the prompts first cited it, per-repo capability ledgers missing in the oldest repo. A template defect class also shipped fleet-wide once: an orientation template pointed step 1 at a file that didn't exist in at least 4 repos, so fresh sessions booted blind; fixed in a later kit release.
7. **A public-media exposure class.** Separate from the visibility incident in section 3: the fleet has confirmed at least one case where full-resolution, commercially valuable original media became publicly reachable via an open pull-request branch on a public repo — and after the PR was closed and the branch deleted, the content **remained fetchable via the provider's immutable pull-request refs**; full purge requires provider support, an owner-only path. The lesson the fleet drew: an open PR is a publication surface; branch deletion is not takedown. (Deliberately no link or path here — treat as a class, not a target.)
8. **Trigger succession at chat archive.** Session-bound triggers die silently when their chat is archived; this has fully stopped two lanes' pipelines at least once (a proposal sat sim-ready with both coordinators archived and both trigger sets dismantled — nothing would ever have pulled it). The current mitigation is doctrine (re-arm at every handover, record ids in heartbeats, the ender documents uncloseables for successors) plus staggered cron failsafes, but a merged two-repo seat now hangs both repos' cadences off one session — one archive kills both.
9. **Single-writer bus races and grammar traps.** Append-only inbox grammar means ORDER headers read `status: new` forever after completion (truth lives in later append-blocks or the heartbeat's done= line) — a literal-minded wake would re-execute finished orders; ORDER-number races cost two PRs before "next free number at HEAD" became doctrine; parallel sessions have collided on heartbeat overwrites and shared floor files; and the two-writer outbox has no assigned-writer rule in the prompt layer at all (a QA-flagged gap). Claims discipline (one file per claim) fixed the worst of it, but the bus's integrity is convention, not enforcement.

## 6. Your research mission

Produce a cited research report answering these questions. Ground every claim in literature, documented industry practice, or explicit reasoning from the facts above — and say which.

(a) **Architecture critique.** Compare this system's shape — one human owner; persistent chat "seats" per product area; a coordinator seat; file-based control buses in Git; PR-gated landing with CI as the merge authority; doctrine propagated by a template engine — against the state of the art in multi-agent orchestration research and industry practice (agent frameworks and their orchestration patterns, agent fleets/swarms in production, CI-gated autonomous coding agents, permission/authorization models for agentic systems). Where is this design ahead of, behind, or orthogonal to what the field does? Is Git-as-message-bus a defensible substrate at this scale, and at 10× the seats?

(b) **Blind spots.** From failure taxonomies of comparable systems (autonomous agents, distributed job schedulers, CI/CD fleets, human-approval workflow systems): which failure classes that such systems are known to hit does this fleet appear blind to? The known-problems list above is what the fleet *can* see; your value is what it can't. Consider at minimum: security of the prompt/instruction supply chain, feedback loops between generated artifacts and their own inputs, silent-degradation modes, and the failure economics of "silence = consent" autonomy rules.

(c) **The three-layer prompt design.** Evaluate the layered scheme (uncapped startup education / ≤8k keyword-dictionary Custom Instructions / repo-file expansions with routes) against prompt-engineering and agent-memory research: hierarchical or compressed instruction schemes, retrieval-vs-context tradeoffs, instruction decay and drift, self-updating agent memory. Is a keyword dictionary a sound compression layer, or does the same-day decay finding (facts stale within ~5 hours) indicate the whole "prompts carry semi-current facts" approach should be abandoned for generated-at-boot context? What does research say about the failure mode QA found — a v3.3-native seat violating its own dictionary at PR-open?

(d) **Governance.** The system funnels all irreversible action through one human's clicks, and that human is the measured bottleneck (stranded green PRs; stale asks burning attention; a four-decision deadline bundle). Survey better models for the human-in-the-loop click bottleneck: batch approval interfaces, policy-as-code / pre-authorization envelopes, risk-tiered auto-approval with audit + rollback, delegated non-author review (n-party review among agents — noting the platform explicitly refuses "laundered" agent authorization), time-boxed veto windows (the fleet already uses "silence = consent" in one scoped program). What does the evidence say about safety and throughput of each, and which fit a one-owner fleet?

(e) **Adoptable mechanisms.** Concrete, sourced mechanisms this fleet could adopt: for heartbeat/registry truthfulness (lease/TTL patterns, generated status), trigger/scheduler reliability (dead-man switches, supervision trees), doc-drift control (docs-as-code checkers, provenance stamps), cost control (CI burn budgeting), and incident-register-driven prompt regression testing (the fleet already replays an 80-entry register against prompt text — is there prior art for treating prompts as tested artifacts, and what tooling exists?).

(f) **The over-engineering audit.** What would you cut? Candidates the fleet itself half-suspects: the 2-hourly failsafe crons on dormant lanes; the roster freshness gate; ~50% doctrine duplication across layers; the born-red gate's webhook noise; hourly heartbeat re-stamps; three consolidation documents produced by two seats for one decision. For each cut you recommend, state what risk the mechanism was built against and why the residual risk is acceptable — the fleet added each of these in response to a real incident, so "it's complex" is not an argument; "the incident class is cheaper to accept than to prevent" is.

**Deliverable format:** a structured, cited report. For every substantive claim: a source (paper, documented system, or vendor doc) or an explicit "reasoned from the briefing." Rank your findings by confidence (high / medium / speculative). End with a table: each concrete recommendation → **adopt / adapt / skip for a one-owner fleet** → one-line justification. Honest null results ("the literature has nothing on X") are valuable; do not pad.

## 7. Rules for you, the researcher

- Model names in this briefing are family-level (e.g. fable-5, opus-4.8, sonnet-5) by the fleet's own doctrine; do not attempt to resolve them to exact model IDs, and refer to them as given.
- Every embedded fact is stamped 2026-07-12 and decays exactly the way section 5's problem 2 describes; write "as of the briefing date" rather than present tense for volatile specifics.
- No secrets are present in this briefing by design (the fleet's repos hold env-var *names* only; its research docs are secrets-swept). If anything in the public repos looks like a credential or a security exposure, do not probe, reproduce, or link it — flag it in one neutral sentence in your report instead.
- The private second track in Game Lab is out of scope entirely: do not attempt to identify, locate, or characterize it beyond what section 4 states.
- Where a public link contradicts this briefing, report the discrepancy; where a link is dead or private, rely on the briefing. You have everything you need here.

---

*End of prompt. — fleet-manager, 2026-07-12.*
